one_line_takeaway: In nonparametric regression, a rigorous minimax theory shows that fitting training data too exactly (small interpolation tolerance delta, as over-parameterized DNNs tend to do) provably worsens the best-achievable adversarial (bounded-perturbation) risk, with a sharp phase transition and a counterintuitive 'curse of sample size' where more training data can make highly interpolating estimators less robust to test-time attacks.
key_finding: Interpolation (near-exact training-data fitting, as DNNs commonly do) is provably and quantifiably harmful to adversarial robustness in this nonparametric regression minimax framework: mild interpolation (delta >= C*sqrt(log n)) preserves the optimal adversarial minimax rate, but as delta shrinks toward 0 (exact interpolation) the minimax adversarial risk degrades from r^(2*min(1,beta)) + n^(-2beta/(2beta+d)) to a much worse rate governed by n*r^d, and can diverge entirely once the attack radius exceeds a threshold of order (n/log n)^(-1/d) -- at which point a bounded-delta interpolator cannot even beat the trivial constant-zero predictor. Simulation numbers (Case 1, n=80): at r=0 the median adversarial loss (SE) is 0.072 (0.021) for LP, 0.072 (0.021) for IP1, 0.072 (0.021) for IP2, 0.093 (0.010) for SI, 0.534 (0.009) for 1-N, and 0.823 (0.822) for MNN; at r=0.05 these rise to 0.158 (0.043) LP, 0.203 (0.043) IP1, 0.489 (0.043) IP2, 0.827 (0.017) SI, 0.959 (0.014) 1-N, and 2.639 (2.155) MNN, showing highly interpolating estimators (IP2, SI, 1-N, MNN) degrade far faster under attack than the non/mildly-interpolating LP/IP1. The 'curse of sample size' is also demonstrated empirically: for Case 2 at r=0.1, LP's adversarial risk decreases from 0.191 (0.023) at n=80 to 0.121 (0.005) at n=300, while SI's adversarial risk increases from 1.233 (0.036) at n=80 to 2.246 (0.026) at n=300 -- more data hurts a highly interpolating estimator's robustness. On Auto MPG (d=2), as training epochs increase from 30 to 1000 the standard risk rises only mildly from 0.595 (0.007) to 0.681 (0.253) (~14%), while the adversarial risk at r=0.1 rises from 1.313 (0.010) to 3.042 (1.213), more than doubling; the epoch minimizing adversarial risk (20) occurs earlier than the epoch minimizing standard risk (30), i.e. optimal adversarial robustness requires earlier stopping than optimal standard-risk generalization.
core_contribution: The paper (Peng & Yang, Tsinghua Univ., arXiv:2601.16070) proves algorithm-independent minimax lower and upper bounds on the adversarial L2-risk of 'delta-interpolating' estimators in nonparametric (Holder-smooth) regression under bounded Lp-ball test-time perturbations. It establishes a matching minimax rate (Theorems 1-5) showing that the achievable adversarial risk of any estimator that fits training data to within tolerance delta is r^(2*min(1,beta)) + n^(-2beta/(2beta+d)) + an interpolation-penalty integral term that depends on delta, the attack radius r, sample size n, and dimension d. The paper identifies three phase-transition regimes (low/moderate/high interpolation degree delta) and shows that in the high-interpolation regime (delta bounded, i.e. near-exact fitting, as in over-parameterized DNNs that reach near-zero training error), the minimax adversarial rate degrades sharply -- becoming Theta(log(n r^d)) or diverging entirely once n r^d exceeds a threshold, versus the standard non-adversarial minimax rate n^(-2beta/(2beta+d)). It also derives a Corollary (Corollary 1) applying the theory to DNNs that nearly interpolate training data (empirical squared error below a threshold), arguing this places them into the fragile high-interpolation regime and lower-bounds their worst-case adversarial risk by the paper's minimax bound. Two novel qualitative phenomena are named: the 'curse of sample size' (in the high-interpolation regime, increasing n from n to n^eta at fixed attack radius r can increase, not decrease, adversarial risk -- e.g. from Theta(log log n) to Theta(log n)) and a partial 'blessing of dimensionality' (the critical attack radius below which convergence holds grows with dimension d, since higher-dimensional inputs disperse more, reducing interpolator variability).
threat_model: Standard evasion/test-time threat model for regression: after training on n i.i.d. samples (X_i, Y_i), an adversary observes a future test input X and is permitted to move it anywhere within an Lp-ball of radius r (1<=p<=infinity, r=O(1)) intersected with the input domain X=[0,1]^d, i.e. X' in X ∩ B_p(X, r); the adversarial L2-risk is E[sup_{X' in B_p(X,r)} (f-hat(X') - f*(X))^2]. This is a white-box, bounded-perturbation, worst-case (not average-case) attack model applied to a regression predictor rather than a classifier -- the same style of threat model used in the adversarial-example literature (Szegedy et al., Madry et al.) but adapted to continuous regression outputs and analyzed at the level of minimax statistical theory rather than a concrete attack algorithm.
limitations: The theory is restricted to nonparametric regression (squared-error loss) with a Holder-smooth true regression function and an input density bounded above/below on [0,1]^d (Assumption 1) -- it does not directly cover classification, cross-entropy losses, or unbounded/heavy-tailed input distributions. The lower bound (Theorem 1) and the sharpest phase-transition results (Theorems 4-5) are proved under i.i.d. Gaussian noise; the matching upper bound (Theorem 2) only requires sub-Gaussian noise, so the exact rate-matching claim across the full delta range formally requires Gaussianity for the tightest lower bound. The constructive optimal interpolator f-hat_{delta,tau} used to prove achievability is not a practical/typical training algorithm (Example 1's tau=0 variant is explicitly noted as non-continuous/non-differentiable and not intended for practical ML use); the paper's link to real DNNs (Corollary 1) is only an implication under a nearly-interpolating condition citing an external result (Kohler & Krzyzak 2021) for when gradient-descent-trained DNNs satisfy it, not a direct analysis of DNN training dynamics or architecture. The paper also explicitly notes its bounds do not rule out that architectural modifications (e.g., modified activation functions per Haas et al. 2023) could recover better standard-risk consistency, and the numerical experiments are limited to a d=1 synthetic study and a single small real dataset (Auto MPG, ntrain=300) with d up to 7, not high-dimensional or deep-learning-scale image/text settings typical of adversarial-example research.

datasets_used: Synthetic univariate (d=1) regression data generated from three closed-form functions (Case 1: x^3 - x; Case 2: x + cos(3x); Case 3: exp(-x^2) sin(5x)), X ~ Uniform[-2,2], noise ~ N(0, 0.5), n_train in {80, 150, 300}, n_vali=100, n_test=100, 100 replications
Auto MPG dataset (Quinlan, 1993), 392 observations, 7 continuous features + 1 categorical feature, target = miles per gallon, n_train=300, n_test=92, 100 random train/test splits
quantitative_results: Case 2, r=0.1: LP adversarial risk decreases from 0.191(0.023) at n=80 to 0.121(0.005) at n=300; SI adversarial risk increases from 1.233(0.036) at n=80 to 2.246(0.026) at n=300 (curse of sample size)

Auto MPG, d=2: standard prediction risk rises 0.595(0.007) at epoch 30 to 0.681(0.253) at epoch 1000 (~14% increase); adversarial risk at r=0.1 rises 1.313(0.010) at epoch 30 to 3.042(1.213) at epoch 1000 (more than doubling)
Auto MPG, d=2: epoch minimizing standard risk = 30; epochs minimizing adversarial risk at r=0.05 and r=0.1 = 20 each (earlier stopping favors adversarial robustness over standard risk)
baselines_compared: LP: local polynomial regression estimator (rectangular kernel, degree 7) -- non-interpolating baseline
IP1: delta-interpolating variant of LP with delta=0.75*sqrt(log log n) (moderate interpolation)
IP2: delta-interpolating variant of LP with delta=0.3 (high interpolation)
SI: local polynomial estimator with singular kernel K(u)=|u|^-0.2(1-|u|)^2_+, exactly interpolates while minimax-optimal in standard risk (from Chhor et al. 2024)
MNN: minimum-norm interpolating two-layer ReLU network with skip connection, trained via norm minimization subject to exact interpolation constraints
